message.content: Dimension: SECURITY / DATA-SOVEREIGNTY / PII. Probe /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay for off-box data paths, credential/PII scrubbing COMPLETENESS (all artifact types, all code paths, all redaction patterns), input validation at trust boundaries. Note: pass-6 found .json transcripts bypassed scrubbing (CV-O-1) — look for any OTHER coverage gap of this class (e.g. the MANIFEST, filenames, other capture paths). If nothing, state what you checked. Return JSON findings: id (P7-<dim>-N), severity, file, line, title, description, poc, suggested_fix. ONLY genuine reproducible defects — do not manufacture nits.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll probe the hc-assay codebase for data-sovereignty, PII/credential scrubbing, and input validation issues. Let me start by understanding the structure and finding the scrubbing code.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git log --oneline -5 2>/dev/null; echo "---STRUCTURE---"; find . -type f -name "*.py" | grep -v __pycache__ | grep -v ".git/" | head -100

content.text: Let me look at the core scrubbing logic and the pass-6 CV-O-1 fix to understand the scrubbing pattern.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
77	
78	
79	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
80	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
81	        if pat.groups == 2:
82	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
83	        elif pat.groups == 1:
84	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
85	        else:
86	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
87	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
88	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
89	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
90	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
91	    return text
92	
93	
94	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
95	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
96	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
97	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
98	
99	
100	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
101	    try:
102	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
103	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
104	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
105	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
106	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
107	        # Decode strictly to classify: text → scrub; genuinely binary (rare here) → copy verbatim.
108	        try:
109	            text = raw.decode("utf-8")
110	        except UnicodeDecodeError:
111	            data = raw  # binary blob — nothing to scrub
112	        else:
113	            data = _scrub(text).encode("utf-8")
114	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
115	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
116	        else:
117	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
118	        return {
119	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
120	            "bytes": len(data),
121	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
122	        }
123	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
124	        return None
125	
126	
127	def main() -> int:
128	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
129	    if not src.is_dir():
130	        return 0
131	    session = _active_session(src)
132	    if session is None:
133	        return 0
134	    sid = session.stem
135	    captured: list[dict] = []
136	
137	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
138	    if rec:
139	        captured.append(rec)
140	
141	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
142	    subtree = src / sid
143	    if subtree.is_dir():
144	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
145	            if f.is_file():
146	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
147	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
148	                if rec:
149	                    captured.append(rec)
150	
151	    try:
152	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
153	        MANIFEST.write_text(
154	            json.dumps(
155	                {
156	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
157	                    "active_session": sid,
158	                    "n_files": len(captured),
159	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
160	                    "files": captured,
161	                },
162	                indent=2,
163	            )
164	            + "\n",
165	            encoding="utf-8",
166	        )
167	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
168	        return 0
169	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
170	    return 0
171	
172	
173	if __name__ == "__main__":
174	    sys.exit(main())
175

transcripts/README.md
transcripts/MANIFEST.json
---README---
# Transcripts (provenance)

This directory captures the **Claude session transcripts** behind the work in this repository,
so the provenance and effort of the agent interactions can be reviewed and audited alongside the
code.

## What's here

- `sessions/<session-id>.jsonl` — the main session transcript (one JSON object per line: prompts,
  tool calls, tool results, model output).
- `sessions/<session-id>/subagents/*.jsonl` — transcripts of subagents spawned in that session.
- `sessions/<session-id>/workflows/...` — workflow scripts and their subagent transcripts.
- `MANIFEST.json` — what was captured, when, and a SHA-256 of each file.

## How it's captured

A committed git hook (`.githooks/pre-commit`, enabled via `git config core.hooksPath .githooks`)
runs `scripts/capture_transcripts.py` before every commit, which snapshots the **active** session
(the most-recently-modified transcript) and its subtree into `sessions/`, then stages it. So
**every commit pulls in an up-to-date transcript snapshot.**

To enable the hook on a fresh clone: `git config core.hooksPath .githooks`.

## Scope & caveats (read before publishing)

- Credential-shaped secrets (API keys, OAuth/bearer tokens) are **redacted** on capture.
- Transcripts still contain **local filesystem paths** and may contain the **operator's email**
  and internal reasoning. They are legitimate provenance, but if this repository is made
  **public**, review this directory first — consider scrubbing further or excluding it
  (`git rm -r --cached transcripts/` + a `.gitignore` entry).
- These files are **not** shipped in the built wheel (only `src/assay_engine` is packaged).
- Transcripts are append-mostly JSONL, so they delta-compress well in git history.

content.text:
The tests confirm scope: they redact email, path-username, slug-username, bare-username — but there is NO test for the operator's full display name ("[REDACTED]"), and the code does not handle it. This is the genuine, reproducible coverage gap of the CV-O-1 class.

I have my primary finding (full-name leak, HIGH — real PII leaking in 5+ committed files) plus the MANIFEST-path secondary gap (LOW — currently no PII in filenames but unscrubbed by design). Let me finalize.

findings.title: Operator full display name (git author) is NOT scrubbed — leaks in committed transcripts (CV-O-1 class)
findings.description: The transcript scrubber (_SECRET_PATTERNS + _USERNAME_RE) redacts the operator's EMAIL and the bare home-directory USERNAME ('[REDACTED]'), and masks the username inside /Users//home paths and project-dir slugs. It does NOT redact the operator's real full DISPLAY NAME ('[REDACTED]'), which is distinct operator PII that appears throughout the transcripts via git author strings, pyproject authors=[], and prose. This is exactly the same coverage-gap class as pass-6's CV-O-1 (a category of sensitive content that bypasses redaction). The leak is real and already committed: '[REDACTED]' is present in cleartext in 5+ files under transcripts/sessions/ (e.g. the top-level .jsonl and several subagent transcripts), frequently sitting directly next to a successfully-redacted email, e.g. `{ name = "[REDACTED]", email = "[REDACTED]" }` and `Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>`. De-identifying the email while leaving the adjacent full name defeats the de-identification (the whole point of #F-024/#G-007 was to avoid publishing the operator's identity). git config user.name = '[REDACTED]' confirms this is the operator's identity, not a coincidental token. No test in tests/test_scripts.py covers full-name redaction, confirming the gap is unintentional rather than a documented scope decision.
findings.poc:
1) cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
2) grep -rc '[REDACTED]' transcripts/  -> multiple files with count >=1 (cleartext)
3) python3 -c 'import importlib.util as u; s=u.spec_from_file_location("c","scripts/capture_transcripts.py"); m=u.module_from_spec(s); s.loader.exec_module(m); print(m._scrub("Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>"))'
   -> prints: Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>   (email masked, full name leaks)
4) git config user.name -> '[REDACTED]' (confirms it is operator PII)
findings.suggested_fix: Derive the operator display name (e.g. from `git config user.name`, and/or a configurable ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES env list) and add it to the redaction set, mirroring _username_pattern(): build a re.escape'd, word-boundaried, length-guarded pattern and apply it in _scrub() (skip if empty/too-short to avoid over-redacting common words). Add a test_scripts.py case asserting `_scrub('Author: [REDACTED] <a@b.c>')` masks both the name and the email. Because the leak is already committed, also re-run capture (or scrub history) so existing transcripts/ files are remediated.

findings.title: MANIFEST.json file paths are written without passing through _scrub
findings.description: main() writes transcripts/MANIFEST.json via json.dumps() with no scrubbing. Each entry's 'path' is str(dstf.relative_to(...)) and the top-level 'active_session' is the session stem; these are derived from source filenames (rel = f.relative_to(subtree)). Today all captured filenames are opaque UUID/agent-id stems with no PII, so nothing leaks in practice — this is why it is LOW, not a live leak. However it is the same structural class as CV-O-1: a committed artifact (the MANIFEST) that bypasses the redaction path entirely. If any future transcript file in the session subtree is ever named with operator-identifying content (a path/slug/username in a filename, e.g. a workflow- or tool-result artifact named after a working dir), that PII would be copied verbatim into MANIFEST.json. The file content is scrubbed (CV-O-1 fix) but the manifest of paths is not.
findings.poc: Inspect main() lines 151-166: the dict (including captured[].path and active_session) is serialized with json.dumps() and written to MANIFEST without any _scrub() call. Contrast with _copy_scrubbed which scrubs file CONTENT but returns the raw relative path string for the manifest.
findings.suggested_fix: Run the assembled manifest dict's string fields through _scrub() before writing (e.g. scrub each rec['path'] and the 'active_session' value, or json.dumps then _scrub the serialized blob before write_text). Cheap, defense-in-depth, and closes the structural gap for future filename shapes.

content.text:
I probed the hc-assay codebase for the SECURITY / DATA-SOVEREIGNTY / PII dimension, focusing on the transcript capture/scrubbing path (the CV-O-1 area), the persistence credential-redaction path, and the reasoning-seam credential scrubbing.

What I checked and found clean:
- `src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py` credential scrubbing (`scrubbed_env`, `ANTHROPIC_*`/`*KEY`/`*TOKEN` stripping) — solid, forward-proof, subprocess env never carries metered creds.
- `src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py` `redact_creds`/`_sanitize_conn_str` — DSN/URI userinfo redaction is sound.
- The CV-O-1 fix itself (`_copy_scrubbed` scrubbing ALL text files, not just `.jsonl`) — correctly implemented; `.json`/`.md`/`.txt`/`.js` subtree files now go through `_scrub`.
- Scrubber coverage for email, bare username, `/Users`+`/home` path usernames, project-dir slug, bearer/JWT/AWS/GitHub/sk-ant tokens — all verified working against realistic inputs.
- Hostname (`[REDACTED]`) — not present in transcripts, no finding.
- Filenames in the current corpus — opaque UUIDs, no PII.

Two genuine, reproducible defects returned:

1. **P7-PII-1 (HIGH)** — `scripts/capture_transcripts.py`: the operator's full display name "[REDACTED]" (git author / pyproject authors) is NOT redacted, while the adjacent email IS. It is already committed in cleartext in 5+ files under `transcripts/sessions/`, frequently right next to a `[REDACTED]` email (`{ name = "[REDACTED]", email = "[REDACTED]" }`). This is the same coverage-gap class as CV-O-1, and no test covers it.

2. **P7-PII-2 (LOW)** — `MANIFEST.json` paths/`active_session` are written without passing through `_scrub`. No live leak today (filenames are UUIDs), but it is the same structural bypass class and would leak if a future transcript filename ever carries identifying content.
